title: Chapter 37: The Price
content: Four cities in nine days. Seattle, Portland, San Francisco, Los Angeles. Each bookstore different — cathedral-ceilinged independents, sleek corporate flagships, a converted warehouse in the Pearl District with exposed brick and craft beer on tap — but the audiences are the same. Packed. Restless. Hungry.

I sign books until my wrist aches. I smile until my face hurts. I read the same passage, the one where Vivienne finds Jax in the server room at 3 a.m. and they don't say anything, they just stand there breathing the same recycled air, and I watch two hundred people lean forward in their seats like the answer to something is hidden in the white space between the lines.
And then the Q&A, always the Q&A, and always — always — the same question.
"Is it true?" A woman in Seattle, maybe forty, her hair still damp from the rain outside. "Is Jax Vance alive?"
"It's fiction," I say.

The audience doesn't believe me. I can see it in the way they nod at each other, the conspiratorial little smiles. They came here for confirmation, and my denial is the confirmation they need. If it were really fiction, the reasoning goes, she'd say it with more conviction. If it were really fiction, she wouldn't look so tired when she says it.
"It's fiction," I say again in Portland, to a man in a flannel shirt who's holding the book like it's a religious text.
"It's fiction," I say in San Francisco, to a pair of college students who are recording the event on their phones.

Every time, the audience doesn't believe me. That's the point. That's the design. The book works because it occupies the space between truth and lie, because it gives people just enough to wonder and not enough to know. The fiction is a shield. The doubt is the armor.

I tell myself this in every hotel room, in every city, lying on beds that all have the same institutional white duvets and the same aggressively neutral art on the walls. I tell myself I protected them. I tell myself the story was the best way to hide the truth — by making it public, by turning fact into narrative, by giving everyone a version they could consume without ever touching the real thing.
I tell myself this, and sometimes I almost believe it.

San Francisco. Green Apple Books on Clement Street, the kind of bookstore that smells like paper and ink and old wood and something faintly sweet, like the ghost of every coffee anyone has ever spilled between these shelves. The event space is in the back, and it's standing room only. I can see people pressed against the fiction sections, craning their necks, my book face-out on the shelf behind them like a mirror I can't avoid.

I read the passage. The audience leans in. I take questions. The first three are familiar — where do you get your ideas, how much of this is based on real events, is Jax Vance alive. I give my answers. It's fiction. It's fiction. It's fiction.

Then a woman in the second row raises her hand. She's older, maybe sixty, silver hair cut sharp against her jaw. She's been sitting with her arms crossed the whole time, and when she speaks, her voice carries the particular clarity of someone who's been waiting to say this exact thing.
"Did you betray her by telling the story?"
The room goes quiet. Not the hush of anticipation — the silence of a held breath.
I open my mouth. Close it. The question sits in my chest like a stone dropped into still water, and I can feel the ripples spreading through me — through my throat, my jaw, the backs of my eyes.

"I —" I start, and my voice catches. I hear myself stammer, and I hate the sound of it, this cracks-in-the-armor noise that tells everyone in the room exactly where I'm soft. "I don't think you can betray someone by telling the truth."

The applause starts before I've finished the sentence. It rolls through the room like a wave, warm and approving, and the woman in the second row nods slowly, her expression unreadable. The audience claps like I've said something wise. Like I've answered the question.

But I haven't. I've said the thing that sounds right. The thing that lets everyone — including me — off the hook. And the worst part is that I'm not sure I believe it. I'm not sure truth and betrayal are mutually exclusive. I'm not sure they aren't the same thing, wearing different clothes.

The signing line wraps around the store. I sign and smile and make small talk, and the whole time the woman's question sits inside me like a splinter that's too deep to reach. Did you betray her by telling the story? Did you betray her by telling the story? Did you betray her by telling the story?

Back at the hotel — the Marker, on Geary, because the publisher is paying and apparently this is what my life is now — I sit on the bed and stare at the wall. The air conditioning hums its low mechanical drone, a sound I've heard in four cities now, the white noise of a life spent in transit. The room is too cold. I don't get up to adjust it.
I call Harriet.
She picks up on the second ring. "How was Green Apple?"
"Packed."
"They all are. You're a phenomenon, Maya. The book's going into its sixth printing."
"I know."
A pause. Harriet knows me well enough by now to hear the seam in my voice. "What's wrong?"

I pull my knees up to my chest, the phone pressed against my ear, and I stare at the city lights through the window. San Francisco at night is a scatter plot of gold and white and the occasional red of a taillight crawling up a hill.
"Someone asked me a question tonight," I say. "At the reading."
"What kind of question?"
"A moral one."

Another pause. Longer this time. Harriet's a lawyer. She doesn't do moral questions. She does legal ones, strategic ones, the kind that have precedents and statutes and neat little boxes for answers. Morality is the terrain she leaves to other people — which is maybe why I'm calling her instead of someone who might actually have an answer.
"She asked if I betrayed Jax by telling the story."
The silence on the line is different now. Not Harriet-waiting-for-more-information silence. Harriet-actually-thinking silence.
"Did you do the right thing?" I ask, and my voice is smaller than I want it to be. "That's what I'm asking. Not legally. I know legally we're fine. I want to know — did I do the right thing?"
Harriet exhales slowly. When she speaks, her voice is careful, measured, the voice she uses when she's being honest rather than strategic — a rarer sound than people might think.
"You told a story that protected your friends and entertained a million people," she says. "That's not betrayal. That's art."

"But what if it's not protection?" I press my forehead against the cold glass of the window, letting the chill seep into my skin. "What if it's exposure? What if I just — dressed it up in fiction and called it a shield, but really it's a map?"
"Then you'd better hope the fiction holds up."
I close my eyes. The cold of the window feels like the only honest thing in the room.
"That's not a moral answer, Harriet."
"No," she says. "It's not. Morality is your department. I just tell you what'll hold up in court."

We talk for a few more minutes — logistics, the next leg of the tour, whether I need her to send the updated contract rider. Standard stuff. The kind of conversation that has edges and corners and can be handled. When we hang up, the room is exactly as quiet as it was before. The air conditioning hums. The city glitters. The question sits inside me, unanswered and unanswerable, and I hate — I genuinely hate — that the applause from tonight doesn't reach me here.

The contract is already on the desk when I find it. The hotel staff must have placed it there, or maybe my editor couriered it to the front desk. Either way, it's sitting in a thick manila envelope with my name printed in block letters on the front, and it weighs more than paper should weigh. It weighs like a future.
I slit it open with my thumbnail.

The offer is for a sequel. Not a continuation — my editor's letter makes that clear. "The market demands the rest of the story," she writes, her handwriting small and precise in the margins of the typed letter. "Readers know there's more. The first book ends on a question. You have the answer. Give it to them."

The advance is seven figures. I look at the number and my breath catches — not at the amount, exactly, but at how quickly my brain rearranges itself around it. What I could pay off. What I could set aside. What security could look like, for the first time in my life, not as a concept but as a number with commas in it.

The publishing house knows. They can't prove it, but they know. The book is too specific, too detailed, too precisely wrong in the places where it diverges from reality. A good lawyer would call it fiction. A smart reader would call it confession. The publisher is betting on both — betting that I have more to tell and that millions of people will pay to hear it.

I could write it. That's the terrifying part. I could sit down tomorrow and write the full truth — not the fictionalized, shuffled, name-changed version that passes through legal, but the real thing. Jax and Vivienne's actual location. The actual mechanism of the hack. The person inside the DOJ who made the case disappear. The trail of evidence that Tom left like breadcrumbs because he was too broken to clean up after himself. I could expose all of it in a way that no legal shield could protect, because the sequel wouldn't need a shield. The sequel would be the weapon.
I set the contract down. Pick it up. Set it down again. The pages make a soft sound against the desk — paper on wood, the sound of a decision being postponed.
And then the thought I've been avoiding arrives, and it arrives all at once, like a door I didn't know was there swinging open onto a room I don't want to enter.
The first book might not have protected them at all.

I stand up. I pace the length of the hotel room — four steps, turn, four steps, turn. The air conditioning hums its indifferent song. The carpet is thick and beige and unremarkable under my bare feet, and I think about Reyes.

Reyes, who is smart. Reyes, who is patient. Reyes, who read my book — I know she read it, Jax told me, a warning passed through the dead-number text chain like a message in a bottle — and who wouldn't read it the way the audiences do. She wouldn't read it for the romance or the intrigue or the frisson of maybe-it's-true. She would read it for patterns.
And patterns are all I gave her.

The fictionalized location that's two degrees off from the real one. The altered timeline that maps cleanly onto the actual events if you know what to subtract. The character based on Vivienne whose disguise is a change of hair color and a different first name — the kind of disguise that only fools people who want to be fooled. I thought I was building a shield. I thought the fiction would be a funhouse mirror, distorting reality beyond recognition. But someone with the right lens — someone like Reyes — could reverse every distortion. Could trace the map backward from fiction to fact. Could find them.
The book isn't just a shield. It's also a key.
A key I wrote. A key I published. A key I sold to a million readers and called protection.

I stop pacing. I press my forehead against the window again, and the glass is so cold it almost burns. Outside, the city hums its own frequency — traffic and sirens and the distant percussion of a cable car bell. None of it reaches me. The double panes see to that. I'm sealed in here with the hum of the air conditioning and the weight of the contract on the desk and the question I can't answer, can't escape, can't even look at directly without flinching.
Did you betray her by telling the story?

I told myself it was love. I told myself the story would hide them in plain sight, that the best camouflage was the one everyone could see. I told myself that art transforms truth into something safer, something controllable, something with a narrative arc that culminates in an ending I get to choose. But what if the arc is a bridge? What if the narrative is a path? What if I didn't hide my friends — I just drew a map to their front door and charged twenty-eight dollars for the paperback?

The contract sits on the desk. Seven figures. The sequel. The full truth. The thing that would make me famous in a way the first book never could — because the first book is a question, and questions fade, but answers are forever. I could write the end of the story. The real end. And it would be brilliant, and it would be true, and it would be the last betrayal I'd ever be able to commit, because afterward there would be nothing left to betray.

I pick up the contract. I hold it in both hands. The paper is heavy and smooth, the kind of expensive stock that publishers use when they're serious, and I can smell the ink — sharp and chemical and more real than anything else in this room.

The money would change my life. The sequel would make my name. And the writing would be — God, it would be so easy. I already know the story. It's been living in me like a second heartbeat since the night Jax Vance walked out of federal custody and into a rainstorm and disappeared. All I'd have to do is open the door and let it out.
But the door opens both ways.

I set the contract back down. I walk to the window and press both palms flat against the glass. The cold seeps through my skin and into my bones, and I stare at the city — at all those lights, all those people, all those readers who love the story and want more, more, always more, and who would consume the truth of the people I love like it was entertainment, like it was content, like Jax and Vivienne's life together was a product to be purchased and consumed and discarded.
The first book was an act of love. I believe that. Or I need to believe that, and I can't tell the difference anymore.

The sequel would be an act of betrayal. I know that. I don't need to believe it — I can feel it in the way my hands shake when I think about writing it, in the way my chest tightens when I imagine Vivienne's face if she ever found out, in the way the word "sequel" sits in my mouth like something I need to spit out.

But the money. God, the money. It sits on the desk in the form of a number with commas in it, and it makes the choice harder in a way that shames me. Because the choice shouldn't be hard. The choice should be obvious. Protect the people you love. Full stop. End of sentence. No sequels required.

But the money is there, and the publisher is waiting, and the readers are hungry, and I'm so tired of hotel rooms and white duvets and the same question at every reading, and there's a part of me — small and ugly and real — that wants to say yes. That wants to be the author of something undeniable. That wants the seven figures and the fame and the certainty of having written the truth, once and for all, with no gaps for anyone to hide in.
That part of me is a stranger. I don't recognize her. But she's here, in this room, breathing the same recycled air-conditioned air, and she isn't going away.
I pick up the contract one more time. I read the number. I set it down. I pick it up.
The air conditioning hums. The city glitters. The window is cold against my forehead.

And the question — the one from the woman in the second row, the one I've been carrying since the first word of the first chapter, the one that lives under every page I've ever written — settles into place like a lock clicking shut.
Did you betray her by telling the story?
I don't know yet. But I'm beginning to understand that the answer depends on what I do next. And next is sitting on the desk, heavy as a future, waiting for my signature.
